title: Charmed Life - Chapter 19 (Fragment 4)
text:
“How did you know I’d got it?” Cat said guiltily, letting go of the dragon in order to get at his crucible.

“The smell,” said Chrestomanci.

Cat passed his crucible over. “Here you are. Have I lost a life over it?”

“Not you,” said Chrestomanci. “But it was lucky you didn’t let Janet touch it.” He stepped to the whirling, and emptied the whole crucible into it. Cat saw the powder snatched away and whirled. The mist turned brownish-red and the humming to a terrible bell note that hurt Cat’s ears. He could hear witches and warlocks howling with horror. “Let them roar,” said Chrestomanci. He was leaning against the right-hand pillar of the archway. “Every single one of them has now lost his or her witchcraft. They’ll complain to their MPs and there’ll be questions asked in Parliament, but I daresay we shall survive it.” He raised his hand and beckoned.

Frantic people in soaking-wet Sunday clothes came whirling out of the whiteness and were sucked through the broken arch like dead leaves in a whirlpool. More and more and more came. They sailed through in crowds. Out of the whirling many, Chrestomanci somehow collected the two Nostrums and put them down for a minute in front of Cat and the dragon. Cat was charmed to see one of his eagles sitting on Henry Nostrum’s shoulders, pecking at his bald pate, and the other eagle fluttering around William, stabbing at the stouter parts of him.

“Call them off,” said Chrestomanci.

Cat called them off, rather regretfully, and they fell on the grass as handcuffs. Then the handcuffs were swept away with the Nostrum brothers and whirled through the archway with them in the last of the crowd.

Last of all came Gwendolen. Chrestomanci stopped her too. As he did so, the whiteness cleared, the humming died away, and the rest of the Family began to collect on the sunny hillside, panting a little but not very wet. Cat thought the garden was probably still spinning. But perhaps it always did. Gwendolen stared around in horror.

“Let me go! I’ve got to go back and be queen.”

“Don’t be selfish,” said Chrestomanci. “You’ve no right to keep snatching eight other people from world to world. Stay here and learn how to do it properly. And those courtiers of yours don’t really do what you say, you know. They only pretend.”

“I don’t care!” Gwendolen screamed. She held up her golden clothes, kicked off her pointed shoes, and ran for the archway. Chrestomanci reached out to stop her. Gwendolen spun around and hurled her last handful of dragons’ blood in his face and, while Chrestomanci was forced to duck and put one arm over his face, Gwendolen backed hastily through the archway. There was a mighty bang. The space between the pillars turned black. When everyone recovered, Gwendolen was gone. There was nothing but meadow between the pillars again. Even the pointed shoes had gone.

“What did the child do?” said the old lady with mittens, very shaken.

“Sealed herself in that world,” said Chrestomanci. He was even more shaken. “Isn’t that so, Cat?” he said.

Cat nodded mulishly. It had seemed worth it. He was not sure he wanted to see Gwendolen again.

“And look what that’s done,” said Mr. Saunders, nodding at the hillside.

Janet was stumbling down the slope, past Millie, and she was crying. Millie handed Fiddle carefully to Julia and put her arms around Janet. Janet sobbed heavily. The rest crowded around her. Bernard patted Janet’s back and the old lady with mittens made soothing noises.

Cat stood on his own near the ruins, with the dragon looking inquiringly up at him from the grass. Janet had been happy in her own world. She had missed her mother and father. Now she was probably in this world for good, and Cat had done it. And Chrestomanci had called Gwendolen selfish!

“No, it’s not that, quite, really,” Janet said from the midst of the Family. She tried to sit down on the fallen block of stone, and got up quickly, remembering the way it was being used when she last saw it.

vocabulary.definition: Make a gesture with the hand to encourage someone to come nearer or follow.
vocabulary.example: He raised his hand and beckoned.

vocabulary.definition: Delighted or interested greatly; enchanted.
vocabulary.example: Cat was charmed to see one of his eagles on Henry Nostrum’s shoulders.

vocabulary.definition: Express dissatisfaction or annoyance about something.
vocabulary.example: They’ll complain to their MPs.

vocabulary.definition: People who attend a royal court as advisors or companions.
vocabulary.example: And those courtiers of yours don’t really do what you say.

vocabulary.definition: A container where substances can be heated to very high temperatures; a severe test or trial.
vocabulary.example: Cat passed his crucible over.

vocabulary.definition: Moving lightly and quickly; flapping.
vocabulary.example: The other eagle fluttering around William.

vocabulary.definition: Wild or distraught with fear, anxiety, or other emotion.
vocabulary.example: Frantic people in soaking-wet Sunday clothes came whirling out.

vocabulary.definition: In a way that shows feeling of guilt.
vocabulary.example: Cat said guiltily, letting go of the dragon.

vocabulary.definition: With excessive speed or urgency; hurriedly.
vocabulary.example: Gwendolen backed hastily through the archway.

vocabulary.definition: Making a long loud cry or sound like that of a dog or wolf.
vocabulary.example: Witches and warlocks howling with horror.

vocabulary.definition: A continuous low sound, like that of bees or electronics.
vocabulary.example: The humming to a terrible bell note that hurt Cat’s ears.

vocabulary.definition: Throw something forcefully.
vocabulary.example: Gwendolen hurled her last handful of dragons’ blood.

vocabulary.definition: In a questioning or curious manner.
vocabulary.example: The dragon looking inquiringly up at him.

vocabulary.definition: In a stubborn or obstinate manner.
vocabulary.example: Cat nodded mulishly.

vocabulary.definition: Breathing quickly and heavily, usually after exertion.
vocabulary.example: The Family began to collect on the sunny hillside, panting a little.

vocabulary.definition: Having a sharp end.
vocabulary.example: She held up her golden clothes, kicked off her pointed shoes.

vocabulary.definition: Behave so as to make it appear that something is the case when it is not.
vocabulary.example: They only pretend.

vocabulary.definition: The remains of a destroyed building or structure.
vocabulary.example: Cat stood on his own near the ruins.

vocabulary.definition: Closed or secured tightly; here meaning made inaccessible or trapped inside.
vocabulary.example: Sealed herself in that world.

vocabulary.definition: Lacking consideration for others; concerned chiefly with oneself.
vocabulary.example: Chrestomanci called Gwendolen selfish.

vocabulary.definition: Disturbed emotionally or physically shaken.
vocabulary.example: The old lady with mittens was very shaken.

vocabulary.definition: Grabbing something quickly and roughly.
vocabulary.example: You’ve no right to keep snatching eight other people from world to world.

vocabulary.definition: Cried noisily, making convulsive gasping sounds.
vocabulary.example: Janet sobbed heavily.

vocabulary.definition: Having a gently calming effect.
vocabulary.example: The old lady with mittens made soothing noises.

vocabulary.definition: Moving unsteadily or awkwardly, almost falling.
vocabulary.example: Janet was stumbling down the slope.

vocabulary.definition: Drawn in or pulled forcefully by suction.
vocabulary.example: Were sucked through the broken arch like dead leaves.

vocabulary.definition: Spinning rapidly around and around.
vocabulary.example: He stepped to the whirling, and emptied the whole crucible into it.
